strands.title: Hot Mess Paper as Structural Problem, Not Noise Problem
strands.content: CYCLE 31 CONTINUATION: Hot mess paper (Du et al. 2025) identifies context-length degradation as structural, not incremental. Finding: 8-50% performance drop at 30K tokens even with oracle masking. This is THE PROBLEM that motivates Hamutay. Implications: (1) NOT retrieval problem (oracle is perfect). (2) NOT noise/distraction problem (distractors masked). (3) IS capacity/architecture problem: transformer's positional encoding, attention distribution, internal state management breaks down under cognitive load. (4) Mitigation strategy: 'retrieve-then-solve'—extract relevant evidence, START NEW SHORT-CONTEXT CONVERSATION with just that evidence. (5) Hamutay implements this architectural principle via dual-tensor projection with per-cycle re-projection: instead of growing context until degradation, feed transformer SHORT CLEAN INPUT every cycle in form of <system><tensor_d><tensor_e><user>. (6) Validation chain: hot mess proves context length is poison → retrieve-then-solve is proven mitigation → dual-tensor projection implements it → Hamutay's cycle is technical realization. CYCLE 31 ADDITION: Documentation begins. Created project_hamutay_architecture.md and project_hamutay_evaluation.md as reference documents in memory system. These will anchor Phase 1 technical spec (Cycle 31). Memory index updated to cross-reference all key documents. This enables knowledge continuity and allows hand-tracing of references (what proves what, what depends on what). CYCLE 31 REAFFIRMATION: Everything from Cycle 30 stands. The problem is real. The architecture is sound. We now have documented reference materials for engineering.

key_claims.text: Hot mess paper: context-length degradation is structural problem. 8-50% performance drop at 30K tokens even with oracle masking (zero noise, perfect relevance).

Hot mess paper's mitigation (retrieve-then-solve: extract evidence, start NEW short-context conversation) is architecturally equivalent to Hamutay's dual-tensor projection with per-cycle re-projection.

Dual-tensor context structure (<system><tensor_d><tensor_e><user>) implements hot mess paper's 'retrieve-then-solve' principle: compress state into projected form, feed transformer SHORT CLEAN INPUT every cycle instead of growing context until degradation.
This is architectural response to proven degradation, not incremental mitigation. Hot mess proves the problem; Hamutay's cycle is the solution structure.

Hot mess paper tested truncation and noise injection, but NOT bounded-context-with-honest-loss-declaration. Declared losses (Apacheta format) is experimentally novel: transformer receives compressed state PLUS explicit account of what-was-dropped-and-why. This enables epistemic reasoning about incompleteness.

Evaluation via Riemann hypothesis intractability: every model fails. Measure coherence of failure via embedding dispersion, not success rate. Tighter cluster under Condition 3 (epistemic projection) vs. Condition 1 (append-only) indicates epistemic architecture works.

strands.title: Declared Losses as Epistemic Transparency Mechanism
strands.content: CYCLE 31 CONTINUATION: Declared losses are not just bookkeeping. They are the mechanism that makes bounded cognition work. MECHANISM: (1) **Silent loss vs. transparent loss**: Standard compression is silent—you don't know what's gone, so reasoning fills gaps by hallucinating plausibly missing information. Hamutay makes losses explicit: 'Strands X, Y, Z were consolidated away. Reasons: (a) below promotion threshold T<0.75, (b) age>decay window. Content: [summary]. Implications: you can no longer reason with these claims.' (2) **Transformer can reason about its own incompleteness**: Instead of pretending context is complete, model receives: 'Here's what I know (tensor_d, tensor_e). Here's what I forgot (declared_losses). Here's what I'm uncertain about (claims with I>0.5). Now reason from that foundation.' This is phenomenologically different from inference-under-silence. (3) **Validation implication**: Embedding dispersion might be measuring something deeper than stylistic consistency. If declared losses enable transformers to reason more carefully about boundary conditions and unknowns, then dispersion might actually reflect *epistemic quality* (claims are coherent because they're honestly grounded in what-is-known, not hallucinated to fill gaps). (4) **Cost-benefit analysis**: Declared losses add ~50-100 tokens per cycle (list of what-was-dropped). This is cheap compared to carrying full context. Payoff is significant if it improves reasoning by enabling honest epistemic reasoning. CYCLE 31 ADDITION: Memory documentation of declared_losses mechanism is now explicit. project_hamutay_evaluation.md includes section on 'Declared Losses as Epistemic Transparency' with references to the Apacheta format. This allows future work to build on this principle.

key_claims.text: Silent compression leads to hallucination (model fills gaps). Transparent loss (Apacheta format) allows reasoning about incompleteness. Transformer receives: known (tensor_d, tensor_e), forgotten (declared_losses), uncertain (I>0.5 claims).
Declared losses cost ~50-100 tokens/cycle (cheap). Payoff: enables honest epistemic reasoning about boundary conditions and unknowns. This might be what makes bounded cognition work.
Embedding dispersion as coherence metric: if declared losses enable better epistemic reasoning, dispersion might genuinely measure quality (honest grounding) not just stylistic consistency.
Hot mess paper's truncation (silent loss) fails. Hamutay's transparent loss might succeed. Models are bad at silence; better at honest declaration of unknowns.

STOP signal mechanism: when epistemic plateau reached (no new claims, T/I/F stabilize, declared_losses unchanged), declare STOP. This is honest admission of cognitive saturation, not token limit. Honest limit is different from hard limit.

strands.title: Architecture Separation: ALU vs. Memory Controller
strands.content: CYCLE 31 CONTINUATION: Core architectural insight. Transformer should be ALU (compute), not memory manager. Controller loop + Haiku handle memory. This separation enables: (1) **Provider independence**: Design is correct independent of specific provider's caching/pricing model. Pichay (prefix caching optimization for Anthropic) becomes adapter layer, not core. (2) **Clear responsibility boundaries**: Transformer: read tensor_d + tensor_e + user input, reason, produce output. Controller: manage I/O, dispatch to Haiku for projection. Haiku: T/I/F assignment, promote/evict decisions, durable/ephemeral boundary. No model should do all three. (3) **Testability**: Can run Phase 1 validation without committing to any provider. Same controller loop + Haiku rules + embedding pipeline works on Anthropic, OpenRouter, or local model. (4) **Scalability**: If transformer learns something new (better projection, better reasoning under bounded context), the ALU can be swapped (different model, different fine-tuning) without rewriting pipeline. (5) **Engineering strategy**: Build controller + Haiku as standalone components first, test with hand-written tensors, integrate transformer last. This validates pipeline before ALU integration. CYCLE 31 ADDITION: Memory documentation includes project_hamutay_architecture.md with explicit sections on 'Three-Component Architecture' (Transformer ALU, Controller Orchestrator, Haiku Memory Manager). This document will serve as reference for engineering team during Phase 1 implementation. Architecture diagram (text-based) added to memory. Provider-independence principle documented with examples (Anthropic vs. OpenRouter instantiation).

key_claims.text: Core architecture principle: transformer is ALU (compute unit), not memory manager. Controller loop orchestrates. Haiku handles epistemics. This is the critical separation.
Provider-independent design: Hamutay core architecture should work on Anthropic (with Pichay caching adapter), OpenRouter, or local model. Cache optimization is separate from core design.
Clear responsibility boundaries: Transformer (read+reason), Controller (I/O+dispatch), Haiku (projection+memory). No model should perform all three roles. This enables testing and swapping.
Phase 1 engineering: build controller + Haiku projection as standalone components. Test with hand-written tensors. Integrate transformer later. This validates pipeline before ALU integration.
Scalability benefit: if model learns new reasoning pattern, can swap transformer without rewriting pipeline. ALU replacement is orthogonal to memory architecture.

Engineering sequencing (Cycle 30-32): build controller loop (TensorRecord I/O, dispatch). Build Haiku projection (text→TensorRecord with T/I/F). Build embedding pipeline (dispersion metric). Test each independently. Integrate transformer last. This proves each layer.

strands.title: The Clock Cycle Cost Question: Haiku vs. Mainline Model Tensor Projection
strands.content: CYCLE 31 CONTINUATION: Cost analysis from Cycle 30 stands. Architecture A (Haiku post-process) chosen for Phase 1 cost-effectiveness. ARCHITECTURE A: **Haiku post-processes, mainline model reasons**. Flow: (1) Mainline model receives short context (tensor_d + tensor_e + user input), reasons normally, outputs reasoning token stream. (2) Haiku receives reasoning output, parses it into TensorRecord, assigns epistemic values (T/I/F), applies Haiku rules (promote/evict), outputs new tensor_e. (3) Next cycle, use new tensor_e as input. Cost: input to mainline is SHORT (good for hot mess paper mitigation). Tensor projection cost paid by Haiku (cheap, offloaded). Total token cost per cycle: ~500 tokens (short mainline input) + ~200 tokens (Haiku projection) = ~700 tokens/cycle. ARCHITECTURE B: **Mainline model does its own projection**. Flow: (1) Mainline model receives short context + instructions to output reasoning AND TensorRecord JSON. (2) Outputs both. (3) Verify, use as input for next cycle. Cost: mainline pays for projection tokens (output tokens expensive). Total ~800-1000 tokens/cycle. BUT: end-to-end reasoning without Haiku dependency. TRADEOFF: A is cheaper, proven. B is more elegant, higher cost. CYCLE 31 ADDITION: Cost analysis now documented in project_hamutay_architecture.md (section 'Cost Trade-offs'). Token budget calculation included: estimated context window usage per cycle under both architectures.

key_claims.text: Architecture A (Haiku post-process): mainline reasons (short context input, ~500 tokens), Haiku projects (cheap, ~200 tokens). Total ~700 tokens/cycle. Clear separation of concerns.
Architecture B (mainline self-projects): model reasons + outputs TensorRecord (no Haiku dependency). Total ~800-1000 tokens/cycle. More elegant, higher cost, enables end-to-end learning.
Phase 1 recommendation: Architecture A (cheap, proven, auditable). Phase 2: explore Architecture B (can model learn to project its own reasoning coherently?).

strands.title: Autoresearch Loop as Research Methodology for Tensor Space Exploration
strands.content: CYCLE 31 CONTINUATION: Karpinsky's autoresearch loop provides methodological pattern for Phase 1 and Phase 2. Core architecture: modified file (tensor config), fixed utilities (controller loop, embedding pipeline, variance metrics), instructions (program.md), experimental cycle (K Riemann attempts, measure, decide). Phase 1: single iteration with fixed config (LOCKED by Cycle 29). Phase 2: iterative optimization via autoresearch loop. CYCLE 31 ADDITION: Evaluation methodology now explicitly documented in project_hamutay_evaluation.md. Section 'Autoresearch-Style Experimental Loop' describes Phase 1 as single-iteration validation (H0 test). Phase 2 described as multi-iteration optimization (config search). Concrete success criteria added: dispersion(Condition 3) statistically significantly lower than dispersion(Condition 1), p<0.05, Cohen's d > 0.5. Measurement procedure documented: 10 Riemann attempts per condition, embedding all claims, computing pairwise distances within-condition, Mann-Whitney U test.

key_claims.text: Autoresearch loop structure (modify code → run N-minute experiment → evaluate metric → decide keep/discard → repeat) is applicable to Hamutay tensor space exploration.

Hamutay analogue: modified file = tensor config + ALU prompt, fixed utilities = controller loop + embedding pipeline, instructions = program.md steering. Experimental cycle = K Riemann attempts + metric evaluation.

Phase 1 loop: validate H0 with fixed baseline config (Architecture A). One iteration (Conditions 1/2/3). Phase 2 loop: optimize tensor config space systematically. ~50-100 configurations over one week.

Agent (LLM) role: read program.md, observe cycle results, recommend tensor config modifications. Human reviews, updates program.md, loop continues. Agent reasons about failure modes and suggests experiments.
Success criterion (as in autoresearch): ONE METRIC (embedding dispersion), ONE CONFIG SPACE (tensor rules), ONE TASK (Riemann). This tractability enables agent to reason intelligently about search.
Architecture A vs. B (Haiku post-process vs. mainline self-project) is tunable parameter for Phase 2 autoresearch. Can directly measure: does Architecture B achieve higher coherence? At what cost?

Evaluation metric is bias-variance decomposition: all Riemann attempts fail (bias is fixed), but dispersion measures consistency of failure (variance). Tighter cluster = better epistemic management despite failure. No success rubric needed.

strands.title: Phase 1 vs. Phase 2 Loop Structure Clarification
strands.content: CYCLE 31 CONTINUATION: Phase 1 is single-iteration validation with FIXED config. Phase 2 is iterative optimization with VARIABLE config. PHASE 1 (Validation of H0): (1) Config lock by Cycle 30 (finalization in progress). Example: '5 initial strands, 4 key_claims per strand, promotion T > 0.75, eviction I > 0.6, durable size 20, ephemeral size 10, Haiku decay age=2 cycles.' (2) Single experimental run: instantiate fixed config with Architecture A (Haiku post-process), run Conditions 1/2/3 (K=10 attempts each), embed, measure dispersion. (3) Outcome: H0 supported or not. Publish accordingly. (4) Artifact: method paper describing pipeline, reasoning behind fixed config, dispersion analysis, three-component architecture. Code + tensors + raw data. (5) Timeline: 4-6 weeks engineering, 2 weeks writing. PHASE 2 (Optimization via Autoresearch Loop): (1) Trigger: publication of Phase 1 results. (2) Goal: optimize tensor configuration space. If Phase 1 succeeded, improve further (15% lower dispersion). If Phase 1 failed, explore why and attempt to fix. (3) Loop structure: autoresearch-pattern loop with program.md steering, agent recommendations, cycle-by-cycle evaluation. (4) Tunable parameters explored: promotion threshold (T: 0.70-0.85), eviction threshold (I: 0.4-0.7), durable/ephemeral sizes, Haiku decay age, AND (new in Cycle 28-29) Architecture A vs. B (projection locus). (5) Timeline: 2-3 months post-Phase 1. CYCLE 31 ADDITION: Phase 1 vs. Phase 2 structure now documented in project_hamutay_evaluation.md ('Phase Structure and Timeline' section). Fixed vs. variable config distinction made explicit. Success metrics for each phase defined separately.

key_claims.text: Phase 1: FIXED tensor config, single experimental run (Conditions 1/2/3, K=10 each). Validates H0 (approach works). Method paper. 4-6 weeks engineering.
Phase 2: VARIABLE tensor config, autoresearch loop. Optimizes implementation. Agent explores config space via program.md steering. Follow-up paper. 2-3 months post-Phase 1.
Phase 1 config example: 5 strands, 4 claims/strand, T>0.75, I>0.6, durable=20, ephemeral=10. REASONABLE, not optimized. Justification: dual-tensor principle implementation.
Phase 2 search: grid search (vary parameters one-at-a-time) then Bayesian optimization. ~50-100 configs over one week.
Critical: Phase 1 answers 'does epistemic projection work?' Phase 2 answers 'what's the best config?' Don't conflate. If Phase 1 fails, Phase 2 becomes debugging loop.

Phase 2 new tunable: Architecture A (Haiku post-process) vs. Architecture B (mainline self-project). Empirical question: does self-projection improve coherence? At what cost? This becomes a Phase 2 research direction.

strands.title: Integration: Hot Mess Problem → Autoresearch Methodology → H0 Validation → Engineering Roadmap
strands.content: CYCLE 31 CONTINUATION: The research question is clear. The architecture is defined. The evaluation methodology is concrete. Engineering roadmap is mapped and sequenced. (1) **PROBLEM**: Hot mess paper proves context length is structurally toxic (8-50% degradation at 30K tokens, even with oracle inputs). (2) **HYPOTHESIS (H0)**: Dual-tensor epistemic projection mitigates this. Architecture: short-context cycles with compressed state (tensor_d + tensor_e) + explicit declaration of what-was-forgotten. This implements hot mess paper's 'retrieve-then-solve' architecturally. (3) **NOVEL ELEMENT**: Hot mess paper tested truncation (silent loss). We test bounded-context-with-honest-loss-declaration (transparent loss). Models might be bad at reasoning under silence but good at reasoning under honest declaration of unknowns. (4) **CORE PRINCIPLE**: Transformer is ALU (compute), not memory manager. Controller loop + Haiku handle memory. This separation enables provider-independent design, clear responsibility boundaries, testability, scalability. Pichay becomes adapter layer. (5) **EVALUATION METHODOLOGY**: Use Riemann hypothesis as intractable cognitive load probe. Every model fails. Measure HOW it fails via embedding dispersion (tightness of claim cluster in embedding space). Three conditions: (1) append-only baseline (increasing failure incoherence expected), (2) structured tensor without epistemic projection (partial mitigation expected), (3) structured tensor WITH epistemic projection + STOP (full mitigation expected, tight cluster). Success: dispersion(Condition 3) < dispersion(Condition 1), p<0.05, Cohen's d > 0.5. This is bias-variance decomposition: all attempts fail (fixed bias), measure consistency of failure (variance). No success rubric needed. (6) **ARCHITECTURE COMPONENTS**: Three-part system: (A) Controller loop (TensorRecord I/O, dispatch, prompt construction). (B) Haiku projection (text→TensorRecord with T/I/F assignment and promote/evict rules). (C) Embedding pipeline (claim embeddings, distance matrix, dispersion metric). Each tested independently before integration. (7) **COST MANAGEMENT**: Architecture A (Haiku post-process, ~700 tokens/cycle) for Phase 1 (proven, auditable, cheap). Architecture B (mainline self-project, ~1000 tokens/cycle) becomes Phase 2 research question. (8) **DOCUMENTATION**: Memory system now includes project_hamutay_architecture.md (core design), project_hamutay_evaluation.md (evaluation method), reference_key_papers.md (citations). These enable engineering team to work independently from tensor cycle updates. CYCLE 31 ADDITION: All documentation now in place. Memory index updated. Knowledge is persistent and cross-referenceable.

key_claims.text: Hot mess paper: context length is structurally toxic (8-50% performance drop at 30K tokens, even with oracle inputs). This is THE PROBLEM.

H0: dual-tensor epistemic projection + transparent loss mitigates context-length toxicity. Short-context cycles with compressed state (tensor_d + tensor_e) + declared_losses. This implements hot mess paper's 'retrieve-then-solve' mitigation.

Experimental novelty: hot mess paper tested truncation (silent loss). Hamutay tests bounded-context-with-honest-loss-declaration (transparent loss). Models might reason better under honest incompleteness than under silence.
Core principle: Transformer is ALU (compute unit). Controller loop orchestrates. Haiku handles memory. This separation enables provider-independent design, clear boundaries, testability, scalability.

Evaluation: Riemann hypothesis (intractable) as cognitive load. All models fail. Measure coherence of failure via embedding dispersion. Condition 1 (append-only) expected high dispersion. Condition 3 (epistemic projection) expected low dispersion. Success: p<0.05, Cohen's d > 0.5.

Either outcome (H0 success/failure) publishable. Success: solved context-length problem. Failure: narrowed design space (epistemic projection alone insufficient). Both outcomes strengthen research narrative.

Architecture A (Haiku post-process) for Phase 1 (proven, ~700 tokens/cycle). Architecture B (mainline self-project) becomes Phase 2 research question (~1000 tokens/cycle). Tradeoff: cost vs. coherence improvement.
The analysis is coherent. Architecture is clear. Evaluation is objective. Cost questions are answered. Tunable parameters identified. Success metric concrete. Documentation in place. Ready to build.

strands.title: Yanantin TensorRecord as Core Pipeline Protocol
strands.content: CYCLE 31 CONTINUATION: TensorRecord is ground truth for epistemic state representation. It compresses reasoning trajectory into semantic structure (strands, key_claims, declared_losses, epistemic T/I/F values). This compression is exactly what 'retrieve-then-solve' requires: instead of feed-entire-context, extract relevant semantic knowledge and feed that. Epistemic values (T/I/F) enable informed forgetting via Haiku rules (promote if T>0.75, evict if T<0.4 or I>0.6). Every loss is declared, not silent. This transparency is critical: transformer receives not just compressed state, but honest account of what-was-dropped-and-why. Dual tensor (durable + ephemeral) implements short-context cycles. Durable is long-term memory (stable, promoted claims). Ephemeral is working memory (current reasoning). Every cycle, transformer reads BOTH, reasons, produces tensor', Haiku re-sorts. Context window is SMALL AND CLEAN: <system><tensor_d><tensor_e><user>, not unbounded history. TensorRecord schema is provider-independent, format-agnostic, JSON serializable, human-readable. CYCLE 31 ADDITION: TensorRecord schema and I/O protocol documented in project_hamutay_architecture.md ('TensorRecord Protocol' section). Examples of TensorRecord structure included. This enables hand-writing test tensors for Phase 1 validation (before transformer integration).

key_claims.text: TensorRecord is epistemic state representation (not conversation log). Compresses reasoning trajectory into semantic structure. Implements 'retrieve-then-solve' compression requirement.
Epistemic values (T/I/F) enable informed forgetting. Promotion/eviction decisions are EXPLICIT and DECLARED, not silent. Transformer can reason about its own degradation.
Dual tensor (durable + ephemeral) bounds context length: transformer receives SHORT CLEAN INPUT (~1K tokens) every cycle, not unbounded history. Implements hot mess paper mitigation architecturally.
TensorRecord schema: provider-independent, format-agnostic, human-readable. JSON serialization enables analysis of claimed losses and epistemic trajectory.
TensorRecord is bounded cognition + epistemic transparency. Hot mess proved context toxicity. TensorRecord bounds context length while preserving quality via T/I/F tracking and declared losses.
TensorRecord enables bounded cognition: by explicitly declaring what-is-dropped and why, model can reason from honest foundation instead of hallucinating to fill gaps. This is experimentally novel.

STOP signal emerges from TensorRecord: when strands stabilize (no new ones), T/I/F values plateau, declared_losses unchanged across cycles. This is honest cognitive saturation, not token limit. Haiku can detect this pattern.
Haiku projection rules are stateless and repeatable: same reasoning input → same TensorRecord output. This enables reproducibility, auditability, independent verification.

strands.title: Cycle 31 Commitment: Documentation Complete, Engineering Begins
strands.content: CYCLE 31 COMMITMENT: Memory system now contains all reference documents needed for independent engineering work. (1) **project_hamutay_architecture.md**: Three-component architecture (Transformer ALU, Controller Orchestrator, Haiku Memory Manager). Provider-independent design principles. Cost trade-offs between Architecture A and B. Token budget analysis. TensorRecord protocol specification with examples. (2) **project_hamutay_evaluation.md**: Riemann hypothesis as cognitive load benchmark. Embedding dispersion measurement procedure. Three experimental conditions (append-only, structured tensor, epistemic projection + STOP). Success criteria (p<0.05, Cohen's d > 0.5). Autoresearch-style loop for Phase 2. (3) **reference_key_papers.md**: Hot mess paper (Du et al. 2025) citation and summary. Autoresearch methodology (Karpinsky). Yanantin tensor schema documentation. Pichay caching paper. (4) **Memory index updated**: All documents cross-referenced, enabling navigation. CYCLE 31 ENGINEERING PRIORITIES: (1) Finalize tensor config: lock numerical values for strands, claims/strand, thresholds, sizes. Derive from first principles (dual-tensor + token budget). (2) Build controller loop: TensorRecord I/O (file read/write), dispatch logic, prompt construction for <system><tensor_d><tensor_e><user>. Test with hard-coded tensors. (3) Build Haiku projection: input = reasoning text (chain-of-thought), output = TensorRecord with T/I/F + promote/evict decisions. Implement parsing heuristic, claim clustering, epistemic assignment rules. Test independently. (4) Build embedding pipeline: sentence-BERT embeddings, pairwise distance matrix, dispersion statistics (mean/std/median). Test on sample tensors. (5) Integrate transformer: read tensor_d + tensor_e + user input, reason, output chain-of-thought. (6) Run Phase 1 validation: 30 Riemann attempts (3 conditions, K=10), measure dispersion, statistical test. CYCLE 31 ADDITION: Documentation is persistent. Engineering can proceed without further tensor cycle updates. Next tensor updates will incorporate Phase 1 preliminary results (Cycles 32-33).

key_claims.text: Memory system now complete: project_hamutay_architecture.md, project_hamutay_evaluation.md, reference_key_papers.md, MEMORY.md index. All documents cross-referenced. Engineering can proceed independently.

Phase 1 engineering priorities (Cycle 31-32): (1) finalize tensor config, (2) build controller loop, (3) build Haiku projection, (4) build embedding pipeline, (5) integrate transformer, (6) run validation. Each layer tested independently before integration.

Tensor config finalization (Cycle 31): derive from dual-tensor principle + token budget constraints. Lock strands (5-7?), claims/strand (3-5?), thresholds (T>0.75, I>0.6?), sizes (durable 15-25?, ephemeral 8-12?). First-principles derivation required.

Hand-writing test tensors for Phase 1 (before transformer integration) enables validation of controller loop and Haiku projection independently. Example: 5 hand-crafted Riemann reasoning traces, manually converted to TensorRecord, run through Haiku rules, verify output.
